question:
Kini ole fa ki Ara omo maa funfun?

Se Mo le se ayewo PCV fun omo yi?

Se malaria le fa ki omo kekere ma ni eje lara ki Ara re funfun
answer:
A child can be pale because of iron deficiency, infections or malaria
PCV can be done for the child to ascertain blood level
Malaria is one of the leading causes of anemia in children

target_text: A mother brought her child to the hospital today, the child is a girl and 6 month old. The mother explained that it’s been up to 2 weeks that the child has been sick, that she has taken the child to a hospital. She explained that the child’s body is very hot, that this past few days, the child’s eyes are pale and the body is pale. When we examined , we noticed that the child’s body is pale, the palms are pale, eyes are pale and the soles of the feet are pale.
reference:
A child can be pale because of iron deficiency, infections or malaria
PCV can be done for the child to ascertain blood level
Malaria is one of the leading causes of anemia in children
hypothesis: Malaria can cause anemia in young children.

question: Iru ogun wo ni a le lo fun ati pe kini imoran ti ale ba lati dena iru ise yi ni gbamiran
answer:
This looks like a case of sexually transmitted infection (STI), possibly gonorrhea or chlamydia, which can cause epididymo-orchitis (infection and swelling of the testes). The whitish discharge and painful testes suggest bacterial infection that needs urgent treatment.
Treatment:
You will need a combination of antibiotics:
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (injection) single dose
Doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7 days
If symptoms persist, you may need further tests like a urine culture or urethral swab to check for other infections.

target_text: A  27 year old man came to the clinic with complaint of whitish discharge from his private part and cannot have sex the way he does before, his two scrotums are paining him, he has been feeling the symptoms for about a week ago. He went to the community pharmacy to purchase drugs and use but didn't feel any relieve.
reference:
This looks like a case of sexually transmitted infection (STI), possibly gonorrhea or chlamydia, which can cause epididymo-orchitis (infection and swelling of the testes). The whitish discharge and painful testes suggest bacterial infection that needs urgent treatment.
Treatment:
You will need a combination of antibiotics:
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (injection) single dose
Doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7 days
If symptoms persist, you may need further tests like a urine culture or urethral swab to check for other infections.
